chunk_summary: This section discusses food labeling thresholds, referral criteria, and systemic pitfalls in managing food allergies in India, emphasizing FSSAI regulations, referral guidelines, and operational countermeasures to prevent economic burden and growth compromise.
learning_objectives: Understand the role of FSSAI food labeling regulations in allergen disclosure and counseling.
Identify referral criteria for allergy and gastroenterology services in food allergy management.
Recognize common systemic pitfalls in food allergy management in India and their consequences.
Learn operational strategies to mitigate economic burden and growth compromise in children on elimination diets.
key_facts: FSSAI regulations mandate allergen disclosure in food service establishments and allow the use of symbols for better understanding.
Referral to allergy services is recommended for suspected/recurrent anaphylaxis, unclear diagnosis, or need for supervised oral food challenges (OFC).
Referral to gastroenterology is advised for suspected EoE, severe non-IgE phenotypes with failure to thrive, or complex FPIES cases.
System pitfalls in India include formula escalation without diagnostic confirmation and prolonged restriction without follow-up.
Children on elimination diets should have growth tracked at every visit, with reintroduction plans documented and eliminations limited to 3–6 months without review.
reference_quote: India’s compiled FSSAI food labeling and display regulations require allergen information disclosure in specified food service establishments and allow allergens to be depicted with easy-to-understand symbols.
document_key_topics: Referral criteria for allergy and gastroenterology
Systemic pitfalls in food allergy management
